parentQuestion: Trauma to a nerve of the brachial plexus may cause wrist drop.

scaffolds.q: The brachial plexus is the nerve network that supplies the arm.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The brachial plexus (C5-T1) supplies the entire upper limb - all movement and sensation in your shoulder, arm, forearm, and hand.

scaffolds.q: Wrist extension means lifting your hand upward (back of hand toward forearm).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Extension straightens or lifts. When you wave goodbye, you're extending your wrist. Without extension, your hand hangs down.

scaffolds.q: Wrist drop means the hand hangs limply because you can't extend the wrist.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! When the extensor muscles are paralyzed, gravity pulls the wrist down into flexion. The hand just dangles - no ability to lift it.

scaffolds.q: The radial nerve controls all the wrist and finger extensor muscles.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The radial nerve innervates the entire posterior forearm - all the muscles that extend (lift) the wrist and fingers.

scaffolds.q: The radial nerve comes from the brachial plexus.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The radial nerve arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus (C5-T1). It's one of the major branches.

scaffolds.q: Damage to the radial nerve causes wrist drop.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Without the radial nerve, the extensor muscles can't contract. Gravity wins - the wrist hangs in flexion.

scaffolds.q: The radial nerve can be damaged by a humeral (upper arm bone) fracture.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The radial nerve wraps around the humerus in the spiral groove. A mid-shaft fracture can crush or cut the nerve.

scaffolds.q: Since the radial nerve is part of the brachial plexus, radial nerve injury IS brachial plexus injury.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The statement is TRUE. The radial nerve is a brachial plexus branch, so trauma to it is trauma to a nerve of the brachial plexus. Wrist drop is the result.

scaffolds.q: Trauma to a brachial plexus nerve (specifically the radial nerve) can cause wrist drop.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This is why the original statement is TRUE. The radial nerve is from the brachial plexus, and damaging it causes loss of wrist extension = wrist drop.
